title: Economic profile: Edmonton (CMA), Alberta - Canada.ca
content:
## Economic profile: Edmonton (CMA), Alberta
Edmonton Footnote 1 has a population of 1,418,118, consisting of 14% seniors (65 and older), 67% working-age adults (15 to 64) and 19% children (under 15).
The key sectors of the economy in Edmonton, which account for 39% of the jobs in the region, are
- health care and social assistance
There are 680,310 employed residents, of which 30% are immigrants. Footnote 2
The economy generates a total of $101 billion in household and business incomes, which amounts to $71,354 per resident.

### General age structure – 2021
% children (under 15)
% working-age adults (15 to 64)
% of seniors (65 and older)
In order to support local services such as health care and social programs and maintain a good standard of living for everyone in the region, it is essential to have more working-age adults than seniors. Edmonton has more working-age adults than seniors compared to the average in Alberta.
From 2011 to 2021, Edmonton experienced
- a 22% increase in population, compared to a 17% increase in the province
- a 26% increase in the population’s proportion of seniors, compared to a 33% increase in the province
- a 5.6% decrease in the population’s proportion of working-age adults, compared to a 5.5% decrease in the province

### Population structure – Changes from 2011 to 2021
Changes in the region
Changes in the province
Proportion of children
Proportion of working-age adults
Proportion of seniors
By 2031, without immigration, Footnote 3 the region could experience Footnote 4
- a 2.8% increase in population
- a 4.0% decrease in the population’s proportion of working-age adults, which could limit the availability and quality of local services
- a further increase in the population’s proportion of seniors by 35%, potentially changing the demand for local services, including health care and long-term care

### No immigration – Possible changes from 2021 to 2031
Changes in the region
Changes in the province
Proportion of children
Proportion of working-age adults
Proportion of seniors
The region relies heavily on working-age adults to keep local services running smoothly and to maintain a good standard of living and economic growth. Immigrants can help by filling job gaps and bringing new skills and ideas, which keeps the local economy strong.
From 2011 to 2021, Edmonton experienced
- a 13% increase in workforce Footnote 5 size, compared to an 8.5% increase in the province
- a 46% increase in the number of immigrant workers, compared to a 42% increase in the province
- a total of 106,698 retirements, Footnote 6 which amounts to 14% of the 2021 workforce, compared to 15% in the province
- a change in the average income per resident from local jobs relative to the province, which is 1.8% lower than the average in Alberta in 2021

Wage income generated from jobs Footnote 7
By 2031, without immigration, Footnote 8 the region could experience Footnote 9
- a 33% decrease in workforce size
- a 28% decrease in the number of jobs
- an increase in the proportion of seniors in the population from 14% to 20%

Unless otherwise noted, all statistics are from the 2011, 2016, or 2021 editions of the Statistics Canada Census, and all calculations are from the Canadian Centre for Economic Analysis.
